title: How to Memorise the Quran: The Complete Beginner's Hifz Roadmap
excerpt: Hifz — memorising the entire Quran — is one of the greatest achievements in a Muslim's life. This step-by-step guide covers daily methods, revision schedules, and tools that top huffaz actually use.
author: QuranBookk
content:
The Prophet ﷺ gave the hafiz — one who memorises the entire Quran — a distinction unlike any other:

<HadithBlock
  text="The one who was devoted to the Quran will be told on the Day of Resurrection: 'Recite and ascend in ranks as you used to recite in the world. Your rank will be at the last verse you recite.'"
  narrator="Abu Dawud"
  source="Sunan Abi Dawud 1464 — Authenticated by al-Albani"
/>

This hadith is one of the greatest motivators for a Muslim beginning the Hifz journey. But motivation alone is not a method. This guide gives you both.

## Who Can Memorise the Quran?

The answer is: anyone who commits consistently. The Quran itself declares:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ"
  translation="And We have certainly made the Quran easy for remembrance, so is there any who will remember?"
  reference="Surah Al-Qamar 54:17"
/>

This verse is repeated four times in Surah Al-Qamar — a divine emphasis that Allah has made His Book accessible to human memory. Age, language background, and prior knowledge are not barriers; consistency is the only determinant.

## Before You Begin: Five Non-Negotiables

**1. Sincere intention (niyyah)**
Memorise for Allah alone. The Prophet ﷺ warned that showing off one's recitation invalidates its spiritual reward. Renew your intention regularly.

**2. Choose one Mushaf and never switch**
Every hafiz recommends using a single physical copy throughout your journey. Your visual memory maps the text to specific page layouts. Switching editions resets this muscle memory. The Madani mushaf (used in Madinah, 15 lines per page, 604 pages) is the global standard.

**3. Learn correct tajweed first**
Memorising with wrong pronunciation means you must later un-memorise and re-memorise. Spend 2–4 weeks learning basic tajweed rules before your first memorisation session. [QuranBookk's Tajweed guide](/blog/science-of-tajweed-recitation) gives you the foundation in structured lessons.

**4. Find a qualified teacher (sheikh)**
The Quran is transmitted mouth-to-ear. A teacher corrects your mistakes in real time — something no app can fully replicate. Online teachers are widely available if in-person access is limited.

**5. Consistency over intensity**
30 minutes daily for 4 years produces a stronger hafiz than 4 hours daily for 6 months followed by burnout.

## The Daily Hifz System

### Morning session: New memorisation

Do this immediately after Fajr when the mind is clearest and most receptive.

**Step 1** — Listen to the new portion 5 times with a master reciter (Mishary Rashid or Mahmoud Khalil al-Husary are widely recommended).

**Step 2** — Read the text carefully, understanding each word's meaning if possible.

**Step 3** — Memorise line by line. Complete line 1 perfectly, then add line 2, then recite lines 1–2 together. Continue until the full portion is done.

**Step 4** — Recite the complete new portion from memory 10–20 times until it flows without hesitation.

**Recommended daily targets:**
- Absolute beginner: ½ page (5–7 lines)
- Moderate pace: 1 full page
- Intensive (with more time): 1–2 pages

At ½ page per day, you complete the Quran in approximately 3.5 years. At 1 page, roughly 20 months.

### Evening session: Revision

<Callout type="tip">
The ratio that most professional huffaz recommend is: for every 1 new page memorised, revise 5–7 older pages. Without this ratio, memorisation collapses within weeks.
</Callout>

Divide your memorisation into two revision pools:

**Recent (last 20 pages)** — revise daily. These are still fragile and need constant reinforcement.

**Older (everything before)** — cycle through in 2-week blocks. If you have memorised 100 pages, revisit roughly 10 pages per day to complete a full cycle every 10 days.

## The "Forgetting Curve" and How to Beat It

German psychologist Hermann Ebbinghaus discovered that humans forget roughly 50% of new information within an hour, and up to 90% within a week — unless the material is reviewed at strategic intervals. This is the hafiz's greatest challenge.

The solution is **spaced repetition**: review new material at days 1, 3, 7, 14, 30, and 90. After 90 days of stable recall, the portion moves into long-term memory. Some dedicated apps like Tarteel and Quran Companion use spaced repetition algorithms specifically designed for Quran memorisation.

## Common Mistakes That Kill Progress

**Rushing new material before old is solid** — Moving forward with shaky foundation pages destroys the overall structure. Never proceed to new material unless you can recite the previous portion fluently without looking.

**Memorising silently** — Your mouth must move. The Prophet ﷺ recited aloud. Muscle memory of the tongue and lips is part of Quran memorisation in a way unique from other texts.

**Ignoring similar-sounding verses** — The Quran contains hundreds of verse pairs that begin identically but diverge mid-sentence. Mark these in your mushaf and drill them deliberately.

**Long gaps from illness or travel** — Even one week of no revision causes significant loss in fresh memorisation. Build a "minimum viable revision" plan for days when life is demanding: even 10 minutes of recitation prevents sharp deterioration.

👉 [Start your Hifz journey with QuranBookk tools](/features)

## A Final Word

The path to becoming a hafiz is long, but it is lived one page at a time. The scholars say: *"You do not memorise the Quran — the Quran memorises you."* Every portion you commit becomes part of your identity, your character, and your relationship with Allah. Begin today, even if only with Al-Fatiha.

May Allah make you among the people of the Quran, who are His people and His elite. Ameen.
